cards.title: Electrotherapy — TENS & IFT

alerts.label: Overview
alerts.text: Electrotherapy uses electrical currents for pain relief, muscle stimulation, and tissue healing. Common in BNYS practice for musculoskeletal conditions and post-injury rehabilitation.

cards._legacyHtml:
Overview
Electrotherapy uses electrical currents for pain relief, muscle stimulation, and tissue healing. Common in BNYS practice for musculoskeletal conditions and post-injury rehabilitation.
ModalityFrequencyMechanismPrimary UseDuration
TENS (Transcutaneous Electrical Nerve Stimulation)80–100Hz (conventional) / 1–4Hz (acupuncture)Gate control theory / endorphin releaseAcute & chronic pain20–30 min
IFT (Interferential Therapy)4000Hz carrier, 1–150Hz beatDeep tissue penetration, oedema reductionDeep pain, sprains, post-surgery15–20 min
EMS (Electrical Muscle Stimulation)30–50HzMuscle contraction, prevents atrophyMuscle weakness, atrophy prevention20 min
Ultrasound (therapeutic)1MHz (deep) / 3MHz (superficial)Thermal + non-thermal tissue effectsTendinopathy, scar tissue, bursitis5–10 min
Wax bath (paraffin)N/A — thermalMoist heat, vasodilationHand/foot arthritis, stiffness20 min
Shortwave diathermy (SWD)27.12MHzDeep heat generationDeep muscle/joint conditions15–20 min

cards.title: TENS Therapy — Practical Protocol

rows: Risk to fetus — avoid first trimester completely
Cannot detect burning — risk of burn

steps.title: Electrode Placement
steps.text: Over or adjacent to painful area (not over: spine, heart, carotid, open wounds, metal implants). Para-spinal placement for back pain. Surround joint for arthritis. Dermatomal placement for nerve pain. Ensure good skin contact — clean skin, use conductive gel.

steps.title: Conventional TENS (High frequency 80–100Hz)
steps.text: For acute pain. Produces comfortable tingling. Patient feels no muscle contraction. Mechanism: gate control — closes pain gate in dorsal horn. Onset: 10–15 min. Duration of relief: during stimulation + 30 min.

steps.title: Acupuncture TENS (Low frequency 1–4Hz)
steps.text: For chronic pain and deeper analgesia. Visible muscle twitching. Stimulates endorphin release. Onset: 20–30 min. Duration of relief: hours to days. More effective long-term than conventional TENS.

steps.title: Intensity
steps.text: Increase until patient reports comfortable strong tingling (conventional) or visible muscle twitch (acupuncture mode). Never painful. Tolerance develops — increase intensity as needed over course of treatment.

cards._legacyHtml:
1
Electrode Placement Over or adjacent to painful area (not over: spine, heart, carotid, open wounds, metal implants). Para-spinal placement for back pain. Surround joint for arthritis. Dermatomal placement for nerve pain. Ensure good skin contact — clean skin, use conductive gel.
2
Conventional TENS (High frequency 80–100Hz) For acute pain. Produces comfortable tingling. Patient feels no muscle contraction. Mechanism: gate control — closes pain gate in dorsal horn. Onset: 10–15 min. Duration of relief: during stimulation + 30 min.
3
Acupuncture TENS (Low frequency 1–4Hz) For chronic pain and deeper analgesia. Visible muscle twitching. Stimulates endorphin release. Onset: 20–30 min. Duration of relief: hours to days. More effective long-term than conventional TENS.
4
Intensity Increase until patient reports comfortable strong tingling (conventional) or visible muscle twitch (acupuncture mode). Never painful. Tolerance develops — increase intensity as needed over course of treatment.
ContraindicationReason
Pacemaker ★Electrical interference — absolute contraindication
Pregnancy (abdomen/low back)Risk to fetus — avoid first trimester completely
Epilepsy (head/neck electrodes)May trigger seizure
Active thrombosis/DVTMay dislodge clot
Malignancy at siteMay stimulate tumour growth
Skin anesthesia at siteCannot detect burning — risk of burn

cards.title: Ultrasound Therapy & Wax Bath

pearls.label: 🟡 Contraindications — Wax Bath
pearls.text: Open wounds, skin infections, peripheral vascular disease (impaired sensation/circulation), active RA flare (heat worsens acute synovitis), diabetes with neuropathy (cannot detect burns), DVT, oedema.

steps.text: Check wax temperature: 42–44°C (never above 44°C — burn risk). Test on therapist's forearm first.
Patient dips hand/foot 8–10 times, allowing each layer to solidify. Creates wax glove of 4–6 layers.
Wrap in plastic bag and towel. Leave 20 min. Remove and discard wax. Follow with ROM exercises immediately after — tissue most pliable when warm.

cards._legacyHtml:
ParameterUltrasound 1MHzUltrasound 3MHz
Depth of penetrationUp to 5cmUp to 2cm
Best forDeep muscles, joints, hip, shoulderSuperficial tendons, face, hand
Intensity (acute)0.5–1.0 W/cm² pulsed 1:40.3–0.5 W/cm² pulsed
Intensity (chronic)1.0–2.0 W/cm² continuous0.5–1.0 W/cm² continuous
Duration5–10 min per area3–5 min per area
Wax Bath Protocol
1
Check wax temperature: 42–44°C (never above 44°C — burn risk). Test on therapist's forearm first.
2
Patient dips hand/foot 8–10 times, allowing each layer to solidify. Creates wax glove of 4–6 layers.
3
Wrap in plastic bag and towel. Leave 20 min. Remove and discard wax. Follow with ROM exercises immediately after — tissue most pliable when warm.
🟡 Contraindications — Wax Bath
Open wounds, skin infections, peripheral vascular disease (impaired sensation/circulation), active RA flare (heat worsens acute synovitis), diabetes with neuropathy (cannot detect burns), DVT, oedema.

cards.title: Thermal Therapy — Hot & Cold Applications

rows: Vascular pumping, oedema down, circulation up
Open wounds, PVD, DM neuropathy, DVT

pearls.label: 🟡 POLICE Protocol — Acute Injury
pearls.text: Modern acute injury management: POLICE = Protection (relative rest, not complete immobilisation) + Optimal Loading (early gentle movement) + Ice (10–15 min/hr for 48–72hrs) + Compression + Elevation. Early controlled loading stimulates tissue healing and prevents muscle atrophy better than complete rest. Ice is analgesic — evidence for actually reducing inflammation is weaker than previously thought. Never apply ice directly to skin — wrap in damp towel. Never >20 min (ice burn risk). Avoid heat in first 48–72hrs after acute injury (increases bleeding and oedema).

cards._legacyHtml:
ApplicationTemperatureDurationEffectIndicationContraindication
Hot pack65–70°C wrapped → 40–42°C skin15–20 minVasodilation, muscle relaxation, extensibility upChronic pain, muscle spasm, pre-exerciseAcute injury, impaired sensation, PVD
Cold pack / ice0–5°C10–15 minVasoconstriction, oedema down, analgesiaAcute injury (RICE), inflammation, spasticityRaynaud's, cold urticaria, impaired sensation
Contrast bathHot 38–44°C / Cold 10–18°C3 min hot : 1 min cold × 4–5 cyclesVascular pumping, oedema down, circulation upSubacute oedema, CRPS, ankle sprainsPVD, Raynaud's, DVT
Wax bath42–44°C paraffin20 minMoist heat, vasodilation, stiffness reliefHand/foot arthritis, stiffness, sclerodermaOpen wounds, PVD, DM neuropathy, DVT
Whirlpool36–40°C warm / 13–18°C cold20 minDebridement, circulation up, wound healingWound care, burns, joint stiffnessMaceration risk, severe cardiac disease
🟡 POLICE Protocol — Acute Injury
Modern acute injury management: POLICE = Protection (relative rest, not complete immobilisation) + Optimal Loading (early gentle movement) + Ice (10–15 min/hr for 48–72hrs) + Compression + Elevation. Early controlled loading stimulates tissue healing and prevents muscle atrophy better than complete rest. Ice is analgesic — evidence for actually reducing inflammation is weaker than previously thought. Never apply ice directly to skin — wrap in damp towel. Never >20 min (ice burn risk). Avoid heat in first 48–72hrs after acute injury (increases bleeding and oedema).

cards.title: POLICE & PEACE+LOVE — Acute Injury Management

alerts.label: Evolution of Acute Injury Protocols
alerts.text: RICE (1978) → PRICE (1990s) → POLICE (2012) → PEACE+LOVE (2019). Each generation has moved away from rest and ice toward early controlled movement and load.

rows: Functional testing, graduated return, protective taping

pearls.label: 🟡 PEACE+LOVE (2019 Update)
pearls.text: PEACE+LOVE further updates POLICE: PEACE (acute) = Protection + Elevation + Avoid anti-inflammatory modalities + Compression + Education. LOVE (subacute/rehab) = Load + Optimism + Vascularisation + Exercise. Key new element: AVOID anti-inflammatory modalities in acute phase — NSAIDs, ice, and corticosteroids all inhibit the inflammatory cascade needed for optimal tissue healing. Inflammation is not the enemy — it is the first phase of healing. Short-term pain control is acceptable; suppressing the biological repair process is not.

steps.title: Protection
steps.text: Relative rest — not complete immobilisation. Unload the injured tissue for 1–3 days only. Prolonged immobilisation causes muscle atrophy (3–8% loss per day), joint stiffness, cartilage degeneration, and impaired proprioception. Use brace, tape, or crutches to unload while maintaining some movement.

steps.title: Optimal Loading ★
steps.text: The most important change from RICE. Early controlled loading stimulates collagen synthesis, tissue remodelling, and neural adaptation. Begin gentle range-of-motion exercises within 24–48hrs. Progress from: pain-free ROM → isometric → isotonic → functional loading. Pain is the guide — stay below 4/10 VAS.

steps.title: Ice (Cryotherapy)
steps.text: Apply for 10–15 min every 1–2 hrs for first 48–72hrs. NEVER directly on skin — wrap in damp towel. NEVER over 20 min (ice burn, nerve damage). Primary benefit is analgesia — evidence for reducing inflammation or improving outcomes is weaker than historically believed. Avoid in: Raynaud's, cold urticaria, impaired sensation, PVD.

steps.title: Compression
steps.text: Apply elastic bandage from distal to proximal (figure-of-8 for ankle). Firm but not tight — check capillary refill and sensation distally. Reduces oedema formation. Compression stockings or taping can maintain compression during activity. Intermittent pneumatic compression (IPC) devices have strong evidence for post-surgical oedema.

steps.title: Elevation
steps.text: Elevate injured limb above heart level for first 48–72hrs. Gravity-assisted venous and lymphatic drainage reduces oedema accumulation. Combine with ankle pumps and active toe movements to enhance lymphatic pumping. Avoid dependent position (limb hanging down) which dramatically increases swelling.

cards._legacyHtml:
Evolution of Acute Injury Protocols
RICE (1978) → PRICE (1990s) → POLICE (2012) → PEACE+LOVE (2019). Each generation has moved away from rest and ice toward early controlled movement and load.
P
Protection Relative rest — not complete immobilisation. Unload the injured tissue for 1–3 days only. Prolonged immobilisation causes muscle atrophy (3–8% loss per day), joint stiffness, cartilage degeneration, and impaired proprioception. Use brace, tape, or crutches to unload while maintaining some movement.
O
Optimal Loading ★ The most important change from RICE. Early controlled loading stimulates collagen synthesis, tissue remodelling, and neural adaptation. Begin gentle range-of-motion exercises within 24–48hrs. Progress from: pain-free ROM → isometric → isotonic → functional loading. Pain is the guide — stay below 4/10 VAS.
I
Ice (Cryotherapy) Apply for 10–15 min every 1–2 hrs for first 48–72hrs. NEVER directly on skin — wrap in damp towel. NEVER over 20 min (ice burn, nerve damage). Primary benefit is analgesia — evidence for reducing inflammation or improving outcomes is weaker than historically believed. Avoid in: Raynaud's, cold urticaria, impaired sensation, PVD.
C
Compression Apply elastic bandage from distal to proximal (figure-of-8 for ankle). Firm but not tight — check capillary refill and sensation distally. Reduces oedema formation. Compression stockings or taping can maintain compression during activity. Intermittent pneumatic compression (IPC) devices have strong evidence for post-surgical oedema.
E
Elevation Elevate injured limb above heart level for first 48–72hrs. Gravity-assisted venous and lymphatic drainage reduces oedema accumulation. Combine with ankle pumps and active toe movements to enhance lymphatic pumping. Avoid dependent position (limb hanging down) which dramatically increases swelling.
StageTimelineGoalsInterventions
Acute (POLICE)0–72 hoursPain and oedema control, protectionPOLICE, cryotherapy, compression, gentle ROM
Subacute72hrs–3 weeksRestore ROM, begin strengtheningHeat/contrast, progressive loading, physiotherapy
Rehabilitation3–6 weeksStrength, proprioception, functionProgressive resistance, balance, sport-specific drills
Return to activity6+ weeksFull function, prevent reinjuryFunctional testing, graduated return, protective taping
🟡 PEACE+LOVE (2019 Update)
PEACE+LOVE further updates POLICE: PEACE (acute) = Protection + Elevation + Avoid anti-inflammatory modalities + Compression + Education. LOVE (subacute/rehab) = Load + Optimism + Vascularisation + Exercise. Key new element: AVOID anti-inflammatory modalities in acute phase — NSAIDs, ice, and corticosteroids all inhibit the inflammatory cascade needed for optimal tissue healing. Inflammation is not the enemy — it is the first phase of healing. Short-term pain control is acceptable; suppressing the biological repair process is not.
